_comment: Chronos Earth — story tours. Each step moves the timeline (year = signed calendar year, negative = BCE; or ma = millions of years ago for deep time), flies the camera to lon/lat/altitude, and shows (or speaks) the text. battleId optionally opens that battle's panel. Add a tour by copying one.

tours.title: The Rise and Fall of Rome
tours.description: From a village on the Tiber to a continental empire — and the long fall.

steps.title: A village on seven hills
steps.text: Legend says Rome was founded in 753 BCE by Romulus, on seven hills beside the river Tiber. For centuries it was just one small town among many in Italy.

steps.title: The Republic is born
steps.text: In 509 BCE the Romans threw out their last king and invented a republic — power shared between elected consuls, a senate, and the people. Its motto: S P Q R, the Senate and People of Rome.

steps.title: Disaster at Cannae
steps.text: Hannibal of Carthage crossed the Alps with elephants and, at Cannae in 216 BCE, destroyed a Roman army of eighty thousand in a single afternoon. Almost any other state would have surrendered. Rome refused — and slowly won the war instead.

steps.title: Caesar conquers Gaul
steps.text: Julius Caesar spent eight years conquering Gaul — modern France. At Alesia in 52 BCE he besieged the Gallic leader Vercingetorix, building two rings of walls — one to keep him in, one to keep his rescuers out. Victory made Caesar dangerously powerful.

steps.title: Actium: Republic becomes Empire
steps.text: After Caesar's murder, his heir Octavian fought Mark Antony and Cleopatra for everything. The naval battle of Actium in 31 BCE settled it. Octavian became Augustus, the first emperor — the Republic was over.

steps.title: The empire at its greatest
steps.text: In 117 CE, under the emperor Trajan, Rome ruled from Britain to the Persian Gulf — roads, aqueducts, cities and law across three continents. Watch the borders on the globe: this is as big as it ever got.

steps.title: Warning at Adrianople
steps.text: In 378 CE, Gothic refugees-turned-rebels annihilated a Roman army and killed the emperor Valens at Adrianople. The legions were no longer invincible, and the frontiers were cracking.

steps.title: The fall of the West
steps.text: In 476 CE the last western emperor, a boy called Romulus Augustulus, was quietly deposed. The western empire dissolved into new kingdoms — but Roman law, language and roads shaped Europe forever. The eastern half, Byzantium, lived on for another thousand years.

tours.title: Napoleon's Wars
tours.description: A young general seizes France, conquers Europe, and loses it all.

steps.title: From chaos, a general
steps.text: France's revolution had descended into chaos and war with all of Europe. In 1799 a brilliant young general from Corsica, Napoleon Bonaparte, seized power in Paris. Within five years he crowned himself emperor.

steps.title: Austerlitz — the masterpiece
steps.text: At Austerlitz in 1805, Napoleon deliberately weakened his own right flank to lure the Russians and Austrians off the high ground — then smashed through their centre. It is still studied as the perfect battle.

steps.title: The fatal march on Moscow
steps.text: In 1812 Napoleon marched six hundred thousand men into Russia. The Russians traded space for time, burned the land behind them, and fought him bloodily at Borodino. Napoleon took Moscow — and then winter took his army. Watch the front line on the globe sweep east and collapse back west.

steps.title: The Battle of the Nations
steps.text: Europe rose against the weakened emperor. At Leipzig in 1813 — the biggest battle the world had yet seen, half a million men — Napoleon was beaten and driven back to France.

steps.title: Waterloo
steps.text: Exiled, escaped, and emperor again for one hundred days, Napoleon met Wellington and Blücher near a Belgian village called Waterloo on the 18th of June 1815. By nightfall his last army was broken — and so was he.

steps.title: The rock in the Atlantic
steps.text: The British shipped Napoleon to Saint Helena, a tiny island three thousand kilometres from anywhere. He died there in 1821, aged 51 — leaving behind a legal code, a redrawn Europe, and an argument historians are still having.

tours.title: The Age of Dinosaurs
tours.description: 180 million years of giants — from Pangea to the asteroid.

steps.title: One world: Pangea
steps.text: 210 million years ago every continent was joined into one — Pangea. The first dinosaurs, like Coelophysis and Plateosaurus, were spreading across it. Look: you could walk from New Mexico to Germany.

steps.title: Jurassic giants
steps.text: By 150 million years ago Pangea was tearing apart and the Atlantic was being born. On the Morrison floodplains, Brachiosaurus and Stegosaurus browsed while Allosaurus hunted — and in Europe, Archaeopteryx, the first bird, took wing.

steps.title: The last kings
steps.text: 67 million years ago the continents are starting to look familiar. In western North America, Tyrannosaurus rex hunts Triceratops, while bus-sized Mosasaurs rule the seas. The dinosaurs' world seems eternal.

steps.title: The day the sky fell
steps.text: 66 million years ago a ten-kilometre asteroid struck the sea at Chicxulub, in modern Mexico. Firestorms, darkness and a years-long winter followed. Three quarters of all species — and every dinosaur that couldn't fly — vanished. Notice the markers are gone.

steps.title: A colder, newer world
steps.text: Twenty thousand years ago, in the grip of the ice age, giants walked again: woolly mammoths and woolly rhinos on the steppe, sabre-toothed cats in the tar pits, giant sloths on the pampas. And this time, humans were watching them — and painting them on cave walls.

tours.title: World War Two
tours.description: Six years that remade the world - from Munich to Hiroshima.

steps.title: Munich, 1938
steps.text: Britain and France let Hitler take the Sudetenland, hoping to buy peace. Within a year, the hope is ash.

steps.title: Poland, September 1939
steps.text: Germany invades Poland with a new kind of fast, mechanised war. Britain and France declare war two days later.

steps.title: Dunkirk
steps.text: Trapped against the sea, 338,000 Allied soldiers are lifted off the beaches by warships, ferries and little fishing boats.

steps.title: The Battle of Britain
steps.text: Through the summer of 1940 the RAF holds the sky. Invasion is postponed - forever, as it turns out.

steps.title: Barbarossa
steps.text: Hitler turns east and invades the Soviet Union along a 2,900 km front - the largest invasion in history.

steps.title: Pearl Harbor
steps.text: Japan strikes the US Pacific Fleet at anchor. America enters the war; the conflict is now truly global.

steps.title: El Alamein
steps.text: In the Egyptian desert, Montgomery's Eighth Army breaks Rommel's Afrika Korps. Churchill: 'the end of the beginning.'

steps.title: Stalingrad
steps.text: An entire German army dies in the frozen ruins on the Volga. The tide of the war turns east.

steps.title: D-Day
steps.text: The largest seaborne invasion ever lands five beaches in Normandy. The Western Front is open.

steps.title: Berlin, May 1945
steps.text: The Red Army takes the ruined capital. Germany surrenders on the 8th of May - VE Day.

steps.title: Hiroshima
steps.text: A single bomb destroys a city. Japan surrenders in September; the war - and 60 million lives - are done.

tours.title: World War One
tours.description: The war that was meant to be over by Christmas, 1914-1918.

steps.title: Sarajevo
steps.text: A wrong turn, a pistol, an archduke. Within six weeks the alliances drag all of Europe into war.

steps.title: The Marne
steps.text: The German sweep on Paris is stopped at the river Marne. Both sides dig in - the trenches will barely move for four years.

steps.title: Verdun
steps.text: Ten months, 700,000 casualties, the village of Fleury taken and retaken sixteen times. 'They shall not pass.'

steps.title: The Somme
steps.text: On the first day alone, Britain loses 57,000 men. The battle gains ten kilometres of mud.

steps.title: Jutland
steps.text: The only full clash of the great battleship fleets. Both sides claim victory; the German fleet never risks it again.

steps.title: America comes in
steps.text: Unrestricted submarine warfare and a leaked telegram bring the United States - and two million fresh soldiers - into the war.

steps.title: Armistice
steps.text: At the eleventh hour of the eleventh day of the eleventh month, the guns fall silent. Nine million soldiers never came home.

tours.title: The Tudors & Henry VIII
tours.description: A schoolroom favourite: the dynasty, the six wives, and the break with Rome.

steps.title: Bosworth Field — a new dynasty
steps.text: In 1485 Henry Tudor defeated Richard III at the Battle of Bosworth, ending the Wars of the Roses. As Henry VII he founded the Tudor dynasty that would rule England for 118 years.

steps.title: Henry VIII is crowned
steps.text: In 1509 the young, athletic, clever Henry VIII took the throne and married Catherine of Aragon — the first of his famous six wives.

steps.title: The Field of the Cloth of Gold
steps.text: In 1520, near Calais, Henry met King Francis I of France for two dazzling weeks of feasting, jousting and golden tents — a summit meant to outshine every rival.

steps.title: Anne Boleyn and the great matter
steps.text: Desperate for a son and in love with Anne Boleyn, Henry sought to annul his marriage to Catherine. When the Pope refused, Henry took a drastic step.

steps.title: The break with Rome
steps.text: The 1534 Act of Supremacy made Henry — not the Pope — head of the Church of England. England had broken with Rome, and the Reformation had begun.

steps.title: Dissolution of the monasteries
steps.text: From 1536 Henry closed the monasteries and abbeys, seizing their wealth and lands. Great houses like Glastonbury were stripped and left as ruins that still stand today.

steps.title: The six wives
steps.text: Anne Boleyn was executed at the Tower of London in 1536. In all Henry had six wives — divorced, beheaded, died; divorced, beheaded, survived — Catherine of Aragon, Anne Boleyn, Jane Seymour, Anne of Cleves, Catherine Howard and Catherine Parr.

steps.title: The Mary Rose sinks
steps.text: In 1545 Henry's great warship the Mary Rose sank in the Solent as the French fleet attacked. Raised in 1982, her timbers and thousands of Tudor objects survive in Portsmouth.

steps.title: Henry VIII dies
steps.text: Henry died in 1547 and was buried at St George’s Chapel, Windsor. His young son Edward VI, then his daughters Mary I and Elizabeth I, would each take the throne in turn.

steps.title: Elizabeth I — the golden age
steps.text: In 1558 Anne Boleyn’s daughter became Elizabeth I. Her long reign — Shakespeare, Drake, the defeat of the Spanish Armada — is remembered as a Tudor golden age.

tours.title: Ancient Egypt
tours.description: Pharaohs, pyramids and hieroglyphs — 2,500 years by the Nile.

steps.title: The Great Pyramid of Giza
steps.text: Around 2560 BCE the Egyptians built the Great Pyramid as a tomb for pharaoh Khufu. At 146 m it was the tallest building on Earth for nearly 4,000 years — made of over two million stone blocks.

steps.title: The Great Sphinx
steps.text: Beside the pyramids crouches the Sphinx — a giant lion with a human head, carved from a single ridge of rock, guarding the Giza plateau.

steps.title: Hatshepsut, the woman pharaoh
steps.text: Hatshepsut became one of Egypt’s few female pharaohs around 1479 BCE, ruling for over 20 years and building a magnificent temple at Deir el-Bahari.

steps.title: Tutankhamun
steps.text: The boy-king Tutankhamun died around 1323 BCE and was buried in the Valley of the Kings. When Howard Carter found his golden tomb in 1922, the treasures made him the most famous pharaoh of all.

steps.title: Ramesses II & Abu Simbel
steps.text: Ramesses the Great carved four colossal statues of himself into the cliff at Abu Simbel. Twice a year the rising sun still reaches deep inside to light the inner sanctuary.

steps.title: The Rosetta Stone
steps.text: Carved in 196 BCE with the same text in three scripts, the Rosetta Stone — found in 1799 — became the key that let scholars finally read Egyptian hieroglyphs.

steps.title: Cleopatra, the last pharaoh
steps.text: Cleopatra VII ruled from Alexandria, allied with Rome’s Julius Caesar and Mark Antony. Her death in 30 BCE ended 3,000 years of the pharaohs, as Egypt became a Roman province.

tours.title: The Victorians
tours.description: Steam, empire and invention — Britain, 1837 to 1901.

steps.title: The railway age
steps.text: In 1830 the Liverpool & Manchester Railway opened — the first inter-city line worked entirely by steam. Railways would soon stitch the whole country together and change everyday life for ever.

steps.title: Queen Victoria
steps.text: In 1837 the 18-year-old Victoria became queen. Her 63-year reign gave its name to an age of huge change — factories, railways, science and a vast empire.

steps.title: Dickens and the cities
steps.text: Charles Dickens’s stories — Oliver Twist, A Christmas Carol (1843) — showed the poverty and hope of the crowded new industrial cities, and helped push for reform.

steps.title: The Great Exhibition
steps.text: In 1851 the Crystal Palace — a giant hall of iron and glass in Hyde Park — hosted the Great Exhibition, showing off the marvels of industry to six million visitors.

steps.title: Darwin’s big idea
steps.text: From his home in Kent, Charles Darwin published On the Origin of Species in 1859, explaining life through evolution by natural selection — one of the most important ideas in science.

steps.title: The Underground
steps.text: London opened the world’s first underground railway in 1863 — steam trains running in tunnels beneath the streets to carry the growing city’s workers.

steps.title: Empress of India
steps.text: By 1876 Victoria was declared Empress of India, ruling an empire so wide that it was said “the sun never set” on it — a source of both pride and, we now see, great harm.

steps.title: The end of an era
steps.text: Victoria died at Osborne House on the Isle of Wight in 1901, mourned across the world. The 20th century — cars, flight and world wars — was about to begin.

tours.title: The Seven Wonders of the Ancient World
tours.description: The seven greatest marvels the ancient Greeks made a list of — and what became of them.

steps.title: The Great Pyramid of Giza
steps.text: The oldest Wonder — and the only one still standing. Built around 2560 BCE as a tomb for pharaoh Khufu, it was once cased in gleaming white limestone with a golden cap. Open it in 3D and step through the years to watch it built. It stayed the tallest thing humans had ever made for nearly 4,000 years.

steps.title: The Hanging Gardens of Babylon
steps.text: Terraces of trees and flowers rising like a green hillside beside the river, watered by machines that lifted water to the top. No one has ever found them — this is the only Wonder we're not even sure was real, or whether it stood here at Babylon or far to the north at Nineveh.

steps.title: The Temple of Artemis at Ephesus
steps.text: A gigantic marble temple with a whole forest of columns, far bigger than the Parthenon. A man burned it down in 356 BCE just to make his name famous — so the Ephesians built it again, even grander. Step through its 3D life to see it burn and rise once more.

steps.title: The Statue of Zeus at Olympia
steps.text: A colossal seated statue of the king of the gods, made of gold and ivory over a wooden frame, so huge that if Zeus stood up he'd have taken the roof off his own temple. It sat where the ancient Olympic Games were held.

steps.title: The Mausoleum at Halicarnassus
steps.text: A towering marble tomb for a ruler named Mausolus, topped by a four-horse chariot. It was so famous that we still call any grand tomb a 'mausoleum'. Earthquakes finally brought it down — step through its 3D life to see it fall.

steps.title: The Colossus of Rhodes
steps.text: A giant bronze statue of the sun-god Helios, as tall as the Statue of Liberty, standing beside the harbour of Rhodes. It stood for just 54 years before an earthquake snapped it at the knees. Open it in 3D and watch it topple.

steps.title: The Lighthouse of Alexandria
steps.text: A stone tower over 100 m tall on the island of Pharos, with a fire at the top whose light guided ships into Egypt's greatest port. It was the last of the ancient Wonders to fall — earthquakes finally toppled it after more than 1,500 years. Its blocks still lie on the seabed.
